§ 11 EnEfG - Einzelnorm

# Gesetz zur Steigerung der Energieeffizienz in Deutschland 1 (Energieeffizienzgesetz - EnEfG)§ 11 Klimaneutrale Rechenzentren

(1) Rechenzentren, die vor dem 1. Juli 2026 den Betrieb aufnehmen oder aufgenommen haben, sind so zu errichten und zu betreiben, dass sie 1.

ab dem 1. Juli 2027 eine Energieverbrauchseffektivität von kleiner oder gleich 1,5 und

2.

ab dem 1. Juli 2030 eine Energieverbrauchseffektivität von kleiner oder gleich 1,3 im Jahresdurchschnitt dauerhaft erreichen.

(2) Rechenzentren, die ab dem 1. Juli 2026 den Betrieb aufnehmen, sind so zu errichten und zu betreiben, dass sie 1.

eine Energieverbrauchseffektivität von kleiner oder gleich 1,2 erreichen und

2.

einen Anteil an wiederverwendeter Energie nach DIN EN 50600-4-6, Ausgabe November 20207 von mindestens 10 Prozent aufweisen; Rechenzentren, die ab dem 1. Juli 2027 den Betrieb aufnehmen, müssen einen geplanten Anteil an wiederverwendeter Energie von mindestens 15 Prozent aufweisen; Rechenzentren, die ab dem 1. Juli 2028 den Betrieb aufnehmen, müssen einen geplanten Anteil an wiederverwendeter Energie von mindestens 20 Prozent aufweisen.

Die Anforderungen nach Satz 1 sind spätestens zwei Jahre nach Inbetriebnahme im Jahresdurchschnitt dauerhaft zu erreichen. Bei der Berechnung der Energieverbrauchseffektivität nach Satz 1 Nummer 1 bleibt der Stromeinsatz von Anlagen, die ausschließlich der Aufwertung der Abwärme des Rechenzentrums dienen, unberücksichtigt.

(3) Die Anforderun

results.title: EnEfG im Rechenzentrum - Was Sie wissen müssen!
results.text:
EnEfG im Rechenzentrum - Was Sie wissen müssen!

Die Anforderungen des Energieeffizienzgesetzes (EnEfG) für Rechenzentren sind nicht nur technische Vorgaben – sie sind ein entscheidender Hebel für mehr Nachhaltigkeit, Ressourcenschonung und langfristige Wettbewerbsfähigkeit. Betreiber müssen sich auf Grenzwerte für den PUE-Wert, eine verpflichtende Abwärmenutzung und den zunehmenden Einsatz erneuerbarer Energien einstellen. Dabei geht es nicht nur um gesetzliche Pflichten, sondern auch um die Chance, durch effizientere Strukturen Betriebskosten zu senken und Innovationspotenziale zu nutzen. In diesem Beitrag erfahren Sie, was jetzt konkret gefordert ist – und wie Sie die Umsetzung strategisch angehen können.

## Das Wichtigste in Kürze:

Grenzwerte für den PUE-Wert in Abhängigkeit vom Zeitpunkt der Inbetriebnahme:

Bestands-Rechenzentren, mit Inbetriebnahme vor dem 01.07.2026, dürfen:

- ab 01.07.2027 einen PUE-Wert von 1,5 und
- ab 01.07.2030 einen PUE-Wert von 1,3 nicht überschreiten.

Verpflichtung zur Abwärmenutzung:

Neubau-Rechenzentren, mit Inbetriebnahme nach dem:

- 01.07.2026 müssen mindestens 10 % der anfallenden Abwärme,
- 01.07.2027 müssen mindestens 15 % der anfallenden Abwärme,
- 01.07.2028 müssen mindestens 20 % der anfallenden Abwärme jenseits des Rechenzentrums wiederverwendet werden.

Stromversorgung aus erneuerbaren Energien, bilanziell:

- Rechenzentren dürfen den Anteil an bezogener erneuerbarer Energie nicht unterschreiten:ab 01.01.2024 muss der Anteil

EnEfG für Rechenzentren: Welche Pflichten jetzt gelten und was die Novelle 2026 daran ändert

# EnEfG für Rechenzentren: Welche Pflichten jetzt gelten und was die Novelle 2026 daran ändert

23.06.2026

(aktualisiert am 23.06.26)

- 3 Min. Lesezeit

Rechtsanwalt

Jasper Stein LL.M. Nachricht schreiben

- FacebookLink in einem neuen Tab öffnen
- XingLink in einem neuen Tab öffnen
- LinkedInLink in einem neuen Tab öffnen
- XLink in einem neuen Tab öffnen
- E-MailLink in einem neuen Tab öffnen

## Inhaltsverzeichnis

1. Anwendungsbereich und Kernpflichten des geltenden EnEfG
2. Bußgelder und Vollzug
3. Handlungsempfehlungen

Mit dem Energieeffizienzgesetz (EnEfG) unterliegen Rechenzentren erstmals einem eigenen energierechtlichen Pflichtenregime. Für Betreiber ist die Lage 2026 besonders herausfordernd: Das geltende Recht stellt klare, teils strenge Anforderungen – und eine Novelle, die in der Ressortabstimmung weit fortgeschritten ist, würde diese Anforderungen wieder verschieben. Wer plant oder in Betrieb nimmt, muss beide Rechtsstände kennen.

## Anwendungsbereich und Kernpflichten des geltenden EnEfG

Das EnEfG ist seit dem 18. November 2023 in Kraft und gilt für Rechenzentren ab einer nicht-redundanten Nennanschlussleistung von 300 kW. Es begründet im Kern fünf Pflichten:

Energieeffizienz (PUE): Für Neubauten ab dem 1. Juli 2026 gilt ein Grenzwert von ≤ 1,2; für Bestandsrechenzentren ≤ 1,5 ab dem 1. Juli 2027 und ≤ 1,3 ab dem 1. Juli 2030.

Grünstrom: bilanziell mindestens

Global Data Center Trends 2026 | CBRE

# Global Data Center Trends 2026

## Limited Supply Through 2030 Will Drive Pricing to Unprecedented Highs

June 17, 2026 16 Minute Read

- Share on Facebook
- Share on Twitter
- Share on LinkedIn
- Email

## Executive Summary

### Hear the Insights: Experts Unpack Data Center Trends

Uncover deeper insights from the report. Digital Realty's Andy Power and CBRE's Pat Lynch join The Weekly Take podcast to discuss data center power constraints, evolving location strategies and international market dynamics.

Listen to the Podcast

- Global data center inventory surged year-over-year in Q1 2026 across all major regions, fueled largely by hyperscale and AI demands. Latin America led inventory growth at 41.3%, followed by North America at 33%.
- Despite substantial new supply, strong demand from AI startups, neoclouds and hyperscalers has driven vacancy rates to record lows in many key markets, such as Northern Virginia (0.3%) and Atlanta (1%) in North America.
- Global power availability and grid infrastructure constraints are impacting development timelines and site selection, especially in established hubs in North America (Northern Virginia, Chicago) and Europe (London, Frankfurt). Local opposition is also limiting the supply needed to meet current demand.
- Rental rates are increasing across regions due to tight supply, high demand and rising construction costs. Frankfurt had the highest European rates ($235 to $2

# CyrusOne completes shell and core of FRA5 in Frankfurt, Germany

Plans announced in November 2021, construction began in December 2023

January 15, 2025 By Niva Yadav Have your say

Data center operator CyrusOne has announced the completion of the shell and core of its FRA5 data center in Frankfurt, Germany.

– CyrusOne on LinkedIn

Announced in a LinkedIn post, the operator said: “Late last year we gathered to mark a proud moment in the journey of CyrusOne FRA5 with a traditional German 'Richfest' wreath-lifting ceremony. This milestone is more than just the completion of a structure, it represents the culmination of immense effort, collaboration, and innovation.”

Plans for the two-building site were announced in November 2021 with construction beginning in December 2023.

FRA5, the company’s fifth data center in Frankfurt, is located in Hanau and is expected to offer 54MW of IT capacity across 18,000 sqm (193,750 sq ft).

The first 9MW phase of capacity was due to be delivered in Q3 2024. CyrusOne has not provided updated timelines.

# Data Centres in 2026: Power Becomes the Decisive Asset-Class Question

Frankfurt carries around 745 MW of IT load, yet grid connections face 24-to-36-month waits and FLAP-D prices rise 12 percent in 2026\. Whoever secures power capacity will control the market.

31 May 2026/3 min read/Data Centre/German Data Center Market 2026

Data centres are, in 2026, arguably the most dynamic asset class in the German commercial property market — driven by a structural demand surge with few parallels. The spread of AI applications, cloud migration and data-intensive business models is pushing the need for compute up more steeply than the industry can build new capacity. Yet the real bottleneck is long since no longer the building itself, but the supply of electrical power. results.text:
Power Grid Constraints in FLAPD Drive Data Centre Investments to Emerging Hubs

Instagram Twitter Youtube Linkedin

# Power Grid Constraints in FLAPD Markets Drive Data Centre Investments to Emerging Hubs

- June 5, 2025
- ROE

Power grid constraints in FLAPD (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin) are leading to investment slowdowns in these major data centres markets.

New investments are shifting to emerging markets like Southern Europe and the Nordics, driven by lower grid constraints and green energy resources.

Dublin and Amsterdam are among the hardest hit, with long wait times for grid connections delaying expansion plans.

Southern Europe (Milan, Madrid) and Poland are emerging as new data centre hotspots.

The shift aligns with Europe’s goals of decentralising digital infrastructure and reducing emissions.

### Power Grid Constraints in FLAPD Hit Hard

Europe’s largest data centre markets—Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin (FLAPD)—are grappling with severe power grid constraints that are slowing the pace of digital infrastructure expansion and prompting investors to look elsewhere.

In the Netherlands, grid congestion has become a major bottleneck, with over 10,000 large users and 7,500 generation projects waiting for grid access. Amsterdam, a key European data centre hub, is facing waiting times of up to 10 years for new grid connections—putting plans for new data centre deployments on hold.

Dublin has also been hit

results.text:
EMEA​ year end data centre report 2025

Insight

30 March 2026

# EMEA​ year end data centre report 2025

Demand outpaces supply as AI reshapes the EMEA landscape

- Proptech
- Location strategy
- Data centres property
- Data centres

Authors

Daniel Thorpe

Key findings

FLAP-D has grown consistently despite regulatory and grid headwinds

Vacancy declines to a new record low, constraining sector growth

The Middle East data centre market is entering a new phase of growth

Europe's powered land cost gap is widening

Want to learn more?

Looking for more insights? Never miss an update.

## Key findings

The EMEA data centre market stands at a pivotal moment, shaped by unprecedented demand, artificial intelligence adoption, and a fundamental geographical rebalancing.

- FLAP-D vacancy hits record low of 6.3%, pipeline is 83% pre-let. Capacity is being absorbed faster than it can be replaced. Pre-commitment is now the only viable route to securing meaningful capacity across Europe.
- Middle East under construction pipeline is double the existing capacity. Nine metros in the Middle East have 1 GW of total capacity, with 2.2 GW under construction and a further 12 GW planned.
- AI could represent half of all data centre workloads by 2030. Neocloud signings for AI capacity almost tripled in 2025 across Europe. Inference is expected to overtake training by late 2026, driving distributed demand into new markets.
- Powered land cost gap widens, accelerating shift to seco

Germany

20 December 2024 • 16 minute read

share

# New developments regarding power supply for data centres in Germany – allocating grid capacity, construction cost contributions and impending grid operator obligations

Written by: Lars Reubekeul Christopher Ollech

A power grid connection with sufficient capacity is key to operating a data centre. The related costs regularly represent a substantial portion of the total investment in a project. While available grid capacity is often scarce, operators also have to consider the regulatory framework governing power grid connections – even during the project development and planning phase.

There are currently several developments in this area that project developers and operators of data centres should keep an eye on. These concern allocating grid connection capacity, calculating construction cost contributions (Baukostenzuschüsse), and the possible cancellation of certain regulatory exemptions.1

##### Moving away from the 'first come, first served' principle – new procedures for allocating grid connection capacity

Update from 6 February 2025 at the end of the paragraph.

Requests for grid connection capacity from data centre operators are increasing, particularly in metropolitan areas. As our latest market study shows, interest in constructing ne

results.title: Frankfurt at the Limit: Power Shortages Slow the Data Center Boom|igor´sLAB
results.text:
Frankfurt at the Limit: Power Shortages Slow the Data Center Boom|igor´sLAB

English

📖 Reading time: approx. 4 minutes · 733 words · 4,971 characters

The rapid rise of digital applications is driving a steadily growing demand for computing power worldwide. Streaming services, cloud platforms, e-commerce, and, above all, AI applications are fueling massive expansion of data centers. Frankfurt, in particular, plays a key role in this context, as it is home to one of the world’s most important internet hubs and has developed a particularly high density of data centers. Yet it is precisely this success that is now leading to a structural problem, as the energy infrastructure can no longer keep pace with the rate of growth.

At the heart of the current situation is the limited availability of power connections for new large-scale projects. The energy provider Mainova estimates that high-capacity new connections on a large scale will not be possible again until the mid-2030s. This assessment illustrates just how heavily the existing grid capacities are already utilized. While smaller projects or those registered early on can still be accommodated in some cases, new data centers with high power requirements, in particular, are increasingly falling behind.

The reasons for this development are diverse and structural in nature. Expanding the power grid requires not only significant investment but also time. New lines must be planned, approved, and built, which involves complex requ
